description:
You are acting as a release-manager who is about to hand deployment metadata to an automated release pipeline.

1. A CSV file already exists at  
   /home/user/releases/releases.csv  

2. Create a directory that will contain all release-artifacts:  
   /home/user/deployment  

3. From the CSV, build a JSON file named  
   /home/user/deployment/deploy.json  

   a. Only include the rows whose **status** column is exactly the string “ready”.  
   b. For every selected row, keep only these three keys in the same order as shown below:  
      • service  
      • environment  
      • version  
   c. The final file **must be a single, compact JSON array** (no surrounding whitespace, no pretty-printing, no trailing newlines).  
      Example of the exact formatting (spacing, commas, quotes) that will be verified:  

      [{"service":"service-name","environment":"environment-name","version":"x.y.z"},  
      {"service":"another-service","environment":"environment-name","version":"x.y.z"}]

      The real values, of course, come from releases.csv and must appear in the same order as they were found in the CSV.

4. Generate a one-line log file named  
   /home/user/deployment/process.log  

   Format that the test-suite will look for (exact spacing, no line-breaks at the end of the line):

   YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM  ready_rows:N  json_bytes:B

   • YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM is the local timestamp produced by the command `date "+%F %H:%M"`.  
   • N is the count of “ready” rows you actually exported.  
   • B is the exact size in bytes of /home/user/deployment/deploy.json (as reported by `stat -c%s`).  

5. When everything is done, display the contents of deploy.json to the console so a human can visually confirm.

You will need no more than 3-4 straightforward shell commands to accomplish all of the above: create the directory, filter & convert the CSV, write the JSON file, create the log.

The automated grader will only check:
• that /home/user/deployment/deploy.json exists, is valid JSON, and matches the exact compact formatting described;  
• that /home/user/deployment/process.log exists and follows the precise one-line format with the correct numbers.

Do not print anything else except the final cat of deploy.json.
truth:
Initial file tree that already exists when the task starts:

/home/user/releases/releases.csv

Exact content of /home/user/releases/releases.csv (lines end with LF):

service,environment,version,status
auth,staging,1.4.3,ready
payments,production,2.1.0,hold
orders,staging,3.0.1,ready
notifications,production,1.9.5,ready
analytics,staging,2.2.8,hold

Rows whose status == ready (and therefore must appear in the JSON, in the original order):
1. auth  staging     1.4.3
2. orders staging    3.0.1
3. notifications production 1.9.5

Consequently, the grader will compare the deploy.json against the following exact byte-for-byte string (no newline at the end):

[{"service":"auth","environment":"staging","version":"1.4.3"},{"service":"orders","environment":"staging","version":"3.0.1"},{"service":"notifications","environment":"production","version":"1.9.5"}]

The expected byte-length of that JSON string is 166.

Therefore the correct, fully-resolved content of /home/user/deployment/process.log (again, no trailing newline) must match the regex:

^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}  ready_rows:3  json_bytes:166$

(Any valid timestamp produced within the grading window is accepted.)

Directory /home/user/deployment/ does not exist before the task starts and must be created by the agent.
